topic: A student finds out their scholarship was meant for someone else
raw_output:
[story 1]

The official letter lay spread across my desk, the crisp paper suddenly feeling heavy and cold under my fingertips. My breath hitched, a shallow, quick intake of air. The lines detailing the financial aid, the prestigious university acceptance—it all swam before my eyes, twisting into a grotesque shape. I reread the section about the scholarship recipient, the name embossed in bold lettering, and a sharp, sickening drop in my stomach made me lean back in my chair, the wooden frame groaning in protest. This wasn't mine. A frantic search through my files began, fingers trembling as they flipped through old application drafts, searching for an error, a typo, anything to explain this monumental discrepancy. Every glance at the document brought a tightening sensation behind my ribs, a pressure building that made it hard to draw a full breath.

[story 2]

I stared at the email, the subject line a cruel joke: "Scholarship Allocation Update." My hands clenched into fists on the edge of the desk, knuckles turning white. The details confirmed the impossible: the funds were designated for an applicant named Clara Vance, a name I had never encountered in my own application materials. A slow, creeping dread began to coil in my chest, a coldness spreading outward from my core. I stood up abruptly, pacing the small confines of my room, the floorboards seeming to amplify the frantic rhythm of my footsteps. I kept glancing at the window, as if the answer might be shining in the distant skyline, unable to settle, the silence of the room suddenly deafening.

[story 3]

When I finally looked up from the printed notification, the fluorescent lights of the library seemed too bright, too harsh. My vision blurred for a moment. I felt a distinct hollowness in my chest, as if something vital had been abruptly removed. I slowly folded the paper, the sound of the paper rustling loud in the quiet study area. My gaze drifted to the empty chair across from me, then back to the document. A profound sense of disorientation washed over me; the solid ground beneath my feet seemed to shift. I picked up my pen, but the ink felt foreign, heavy with an unspoken realization that settled deep in my gut, a heavy, inescapable weight.
